---
title: "Conventions"
---

This page records the cross-cutting conventions used in the code and the docs.

## Frames

- `G` means global frame. In this project it is a map-local Cartesian frame in meters. [@nuscenes-issue-56; @nuscenes-issue-108; @nuscenes-issue-122; @nuscenes-issue-699]
- `I` means IMU frame.
- `B` means body or ego frame.
- `A` means gravity-aligned frame with arbitrary yaw. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]

In this project the IMU and body frames are treated as collocated, so code usually uses `I` unless the distinction matters. [@caesar2020nuscenes; @nuscenes-issue-108; @nuscenes-issue-56; @nuscenes-issue-699; @nuscenes-issue-1066]

For the nuScenes-shaped inputs used here, the body and IMU frames both use the usual Forward-Left-Up axis convention:

- `x` forward
- `y` left
- `z` up

The body reference is the vehicle reference near the midpoint of the rear axle. For this project, the IMU reference is treated the same way. [@caesar2020nuscenes; @nuscenes-issue-108; @nuscenes-issue-56; @nuscenes-issue-699; @nuscenes-issue-1066]

## Frame notation and code names

In the docs, a vector carries the frame of its coordinates as a left superscript. A transform carries the target frame as a left superscript and the source frame as a right subscript.

In code, the same quantities appear in compact variable names.

Examples:

- ${}^{A}q_{\fsub{I}}$ in the docs is `q_AI` in code
- ${}^{G}q_{\fsub{I}}$ in the docs is `q_GI` in code
- ${}^{I}q_{\fsub{A}}$ in the docs is `q_IA` in code
- ${}^{G}R_I$ in the docs is `R_GI` in code
- ${}^{A}\mathbf{v}$ in the docs is `v_A` in code
- ${}^{G}\mathbf{p}_0$ in the docs is `p0_G` in code
- ${}^{G}\mathbf{v}_0$ in the docs is `v0_G` in code

A practical reading rule is: target frame on the left, source frame on the right.

If ${}^{I}\mathbf{v}$ is a vector expressed in frame `I`, then the docs write:

$$
{}^{A}\mathbf{v} = {}^{A}R_I \, {}^{I}\mathbf{v}
$$

and the corresponding code might look like:

```cpp
const Eigen::Vector3d v_A = q_AI * v_I;
```

In both cases, the same physical vector is being re-expressed in frame `A`.

Composition follows the usual chained-frame rule:

$$
{}^{X}q_{\fsub{Z}} = {}^{X}q_{\fsub{Y}} \otimes {}^{Y}q_{\fsub{Z}},
\qquad
{}^{X}R_Z = {}^{X}R_Y \, {}^{Y}R_Z
$$

For a unit quaternion, the inverse is the conjugate:

$$
{}^{Y}q_{\fsub{X}} = ({}^{X}q_{\fsub{Y}})^{-1} = ({}^{X}q_{\fsub{Y}})^*
$$

The docs use passive coordinate-transform notation. The quaternion and matrix forms say the same thing:

$$
(0, {}^{A}\mathbf{v}) = {}^{A}q_{\fsub{I}} \otimes (0, {}^{I}\mathbf{v}) \otimes {}^{I}q_{\fsub{A}}
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
{}^{A}\mathbf{v} = {}^{A}R_I \, {}^{I}\mathbf{v}
$$

## Vector names

In the docs, vectors carry their expression frame as a left superscript.

Examples:

- ${}^{A}\mathbf{g}$
- ${}^{I}\mathbf{g}$
- ${}^{G}\mathbf{p}_0$
- ${}^{G}\mathbf{v}_0$

In code, the same quantities appear as `g_A`, `g_I`, `p0_G`, and `v0_G`.

The trailing `0` in names like `p0_G` is a time or index label. The trailing `G` is still the expression frame.

## Points and free vectors

Points and free vectors do not behave the same way under a change of frame.

- Points pick up both rotation and translation.
- Free vectors pick up rotation only.

Displacement vectors are differences of points, so the translation part cancels out and only the rotation remains.

That is the convention behind the usual physical vectors in this repo, such as:

- `omega`
- `f`
- `g`
- `v`
- `a`

These are Newtonian vectors, so they are re-expressed by rotation only.

## Quaternion storage

Quaternion arrays read from JSON use `[w, x, y, z]` order. [@nuscenes-schema; @nuimages-schema]

That same order is used when constructing an Eigen quaternion:

```cpp
const Eigen::Quaterniond q(w, x, y, z);
```

## Gravity and sign choices

Frame `A` is gravity-aligned with `+z` up.

So the gravity vector expressed in `A` is:

$$
{}^{A}\mathbf{g} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -g \end{bmatrix}
$$

The IMU quaternion ${}^{A}q_{\fsub{I}}$ provides the gravity-aligned reference frame used for tilt. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]

Quaternion multiplication uses the Hamilton convention.
